title: Metastelma palustre
intro: Metastelma palustre, also known as swamp Metastelma or swamp gentian, is a perennial plant species belonging to the Apocynaceae family. The plant has been used in traditional medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin infections, due to its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. Bioactive constituents of Metastelma palustre include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In traditional herbal medicine, Metastelma palustre is often prepared as a decoction or tincture, and is used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues.
taxonomy: Metastelma palustre is classified within the family Apocynaceae, commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. This family consists of about 5,000 species, including various types of perennials, shrubs, and trees. Within the Apocynaceae family, Metastelma palustre is further classified under the subfamily Asclepiadoideae, which contains the milkweed and swallowwort genera. The subfamily Asclepiadoideae is characterized by its unique flowers with a five-lobed corolla and a distinctive shape. Metastelma palustre's classification reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the Apocynaceae family.
uses: Metastelma palustre is traditionally used in folk medicine for its purported health benefits. The plant's extracts have been studied for their potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which could contribute to its medicinal uses. In traditional medicine, Metastelma palustre is sometimes used to treat various ailments, including fever and respiratory issues. Metastelma palustre's medicinal uses are still largely anecdotal and not extensively scientifically validated.
